1.synthesis: Psalm 120 is the first of the fifteen Songs of Ascents (Pss 120–134), a collection of pilgrimage psalms sung as worshippers traveled to Jerusalem for the great festivals. This opening song is a lament of displaced longing, beginning with a testimony of answered prayer: “In my distress I called to the Lord, and he answered me. Deliver me, O Lord, from lying lips, from a deceitful tongue” (Ps 120:1–2). The psalmist turns on his enemies with fierce questions: “What shall be given to you, and what more shall be done to you, you deceitful tongue? A warrior’s sharp arrows, with glowing coals of the broom tree!” (120:3–4). The geographical location of the complaint intensifies its pathos: “Woe to me, that I sojourn in Meshech, that I dwell among the tents of Kedar!” (120:5). The psalm closes with the sharpest of contrasts: “I am for peace, but when I speak, they are for war!” (120:7). The one who would ascend to YHWH’s presence begins the journey from a place of alienation among people who hate truth and love conflict.
1.historical_context: The Songs of Ascents (Pss 120–134) were sung by pilgrims traveling to Jerusalem for the three annual festivals (Passover, Weeks, Tabernacles; cf. Deut 16:16). The collection opens not in Jerusalem but in exile: Meshech and Kedar (120:5) are both distant from the holy city. Meshech is a tribe in the far north (cf. Gen 10:2; Ezek 38:2); Kedar is an Arabian tribe to the south (Gen 25:13). Together they mark the full compass of alien territory—north and south, as far from Zion as possible. The pairing may be figurative rather than literal: the psalmist uses the names of hostile peoples to describe the experience of living among those who are fundamentally at odds with YHWH’s ways. The lying tongue and deceitful speech (120:2) are not merely personal offenses but theological violations: in a community shaped by the God of truth, deceit is a form of idolatry.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 120 moves in three movements: testimony of answered prayer and petition (vv. 1–2), taunt against the enemy tongue (vv. 3–4), and lament of present location with the closing contrast (vv. 5–7). Its position as the first of the Ascent psalms is significant: the collection begins with a person not yet in Jerusalem, not yet at the temple, living among hostile peoples—and crying to YHWH from that distance. The whole trajectory of Pss 120–134 is the movement toward Zion, and this psalm establishes what is being left behind: the world of lying tongues and war-love from which the pilgrim ascends. The final verse’s contrast—“I am for peace, but they are for war”—frames the pilgrim as a peace-seeker in a violent world, whose true home is the city of the great King.
1.theological_application: The opening claim that YHWH “answered me” (120:1) in past distress grounds the present petition in historical experience: this is not the first time the psalmist has cried from alienation, and not the first time YHWH has responded. Communities shaped by Psalm 120 learn to begin the ascent toward God’s presence from wherever they actually are—not from the idealized starting point of peaceful surroundings but from the real location of conflict, deceit, and longing. The distress that precedes the cry and the answer that follows it form the grammar of prayer available even in Meshech. The contrast between peace-orientation (“I am for peace”) and the violence of those around sets the terms for all who live as pilgrims: dwelling in a world that is for war while holding to the peace that Jerusalem represents.
1.christology: “I am for peace, but when I speak, they are for war” (120:7) is the posture of Christ in his ministry: he came announcing peace—“Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you” (John 14:27)—and was met with hostility that culminated in the cross. Christ himself was the supreme alien in a world of lying tongues: before Pilate, false witnesses testified against him (Matt 26:60); those around him were for war even as he was for peace. The pilgrim journey that begins in Psalm 120 and ends at the temple mirrors the Incarnation’s arc: the Son coming from far outside human hostility and ascending through it to the Father’s presence, the ultimate Zion where every lie is silenced and peace reigns without end.
